15: A fulfillment (first promise, final fulfillment): 'He shall bruise your head and you shall bruise his heel.' The protoevangelium is the most foundational Christological promise in Scripture — the first statement that the serpent's work will be undone by a coming seed of the woman. The bruising of the heel (the crucifixion, the apparent defeat of Christ) and the crushing of the head (the resurrection that undoes the devil's power over death, Hebrews 2:14) are both within the scope of the single promise. Christ's entire work — Incarnation (born of woman), crucifixion (heel-bruise), and resurrection (head-crushing) — fulfills what Genesis 3:15 compressed into one prophetic sentence.
1: The serpent — “more crafty than any of the wild animals” — introduces the adversary who will be defeated by the seed of the woman (v15). The NT identifies the serpent with the devil (Revelation 12:9 — “that ancient serpent called the devil”; Genesis 20:2) and with the father of lies (John 8:44). Christ comes specifically to destroy the works of the devil (1 John 3:8) and to defeat the one who “holds the power of death” (Hebrews 2:14). The adversary who appears at the beginning of the fall narrative is the same being whose final defeat is narrated in Revelation 20:10. Christ is the answer to the serpent’s question: God has indeed spoken, and his Word is Christ.
2: The woman’s accurate recitation of God’s command before the serpent’s distortion underscores that the fall is not due to ignorance but to deception overcome by desire. Paul warns in 2 Corinthians 11:3 that the church can be similarly “led astray from your sincere and pure devotion to Christ, just as Eve was deceived by the serpent’s cunning.” The antidote to the deception that began here is Christ, who is “the truth” (John 14:6) and in whom “all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” are hidden (Colossians 2:3).
3: The woman adds “you must not touch it” — a slight expansion of God’s actual command (Genesis 2:17). Whether this is her own addition or Moses’aimless harmonization, it illustrates the susceptibility of the divine word to distortion under pressure. Christ as the Word of God (John 1:1) is the revelation that cannot be distorted or added to: he does not merely transmit God’s word but is God’s Word in person. The long story of human distortion of what God said ends in Christ, who is both the Word and its perfect fulfillment (Matthew 5:17).
4: The direct denial — “You will not certainly die” — is the first lie in Scripture and the foundation of all subsequent rebellion against God. Christ identifies the devil as “a murderer from the beginning, not holding to the truth, for there is no truth in him. When he lies, he speaks his native language, for he is a liar and the father of lies” (John 8:44). The lie that death is not the consequence of sin is the lie that Christ’s death and resurrection definitively refutes: he died the death that sin requires, and rose as the first-fruits of those who will not ultimately die (1 Corinthians 15:20).
5: The serpent’s temptation — “you will be like God, knowing good and evil” — promises what only God can legitimately give, through a path God has forbidden. The lie is that autonomy and self-divinization are the path to divine-likeness. Christ reverses this by the opposite path: he did not “consider equality with God something to be grasped, but made himself nothing” (Philippians 2:6–7). The one who is fully God humbled himself to the point of death; humanity’s attempt to grasp divine-likeness through disobedience is answered by God’s gift of divine-likeness through the obedience of his Son.
6: The fall: the woman saw, desired, took, ate, gave. James 1:14–15 describes the same sequence: desire, sin, death. Paul in Romans 5:12–19 frames the entire history of sin and redemption as Adam’s one act of disobedience versus Christ’s one act of obedience: “For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous.” The act narrated in this verse is the act that Christ’s obedience reverses. The second Adam does not take what he was not given but receives from the Father everything that the first Adam seized and lost.
7: “The eyes of both of them were opened, and they realized they were naked.” The nakedness and shame that enter here are removed by Christ. Isaiah pictures salvation as being clothed: “he has clothed me with garments of salvation and arrayed me in a robe of his righteousness” (Isaiah 61:10). Paul applies this in Galatians 3:27: “all of you who were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ.” The fig leaves with which Adam and Eve cover themselves are the type of human self-righteousness — inadequate, temporary coverings that God will replace with his own provision (v21).
8: God walks in the garden and the humans hide. The God who walks with his people is the God who will ultimately tabernacle among them in Christ (John 1:14 — eskēnōsen, “tabernacled/pitched his tent among us”). But the fellowship is broken here by sin; the walking God now elicits fear and hiding rather than welcome. Christ’s mission is to seek and save the lost (Luke 19:10) — to come looking for those who have hidden themselves, as God comes looking for Adam. The divine initiative of seeking the fallen begins in this verse and culminates in the incarnation.
9: “Where are you?” — God’s first question in Scripture is directed to the one hiding from him. This is not a request for information (God knows where Adam is) but the voice of a seeking God calling the lost to come out of hiding. The Good Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the one lost sheep (Luke 15:4–7) is the same God who walks through the garden calling “where are you?” The seeking character of the divine is revealed here and fully embodied in Christ, who “came to seek and to save the lost” (Luke 19:10).
10: “I was afraid because I was naked.” The fear that sin introduces into the human-divine relationship is precisely what Christ’s perfect love casts out: “there is no fear in love. But perfect love drives out fear, because fear has to do with punishment” (1 John 4:18). Paul declares: “there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1). The fear and hiding of Adam before the holy God is the condition that Christ’s atoning work removes — those in Christ approach God not in fear but “with confidence” (parrēsia, Hebrews 10:19).
11: God’s questions expose the truth: “Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree?” Christ as the one who searches hearts (Revelation 2:23; John 2:25) and who judges rightly continues this divine function of exposure and truth-telling. But his exposure is not simply condemnation — he exposes sin so that it can be forgiven and healed. John 3:20–21 contrasts those who hate the light (because their deeds will be exposed) with those who come to the light (because their deeds are done in God). Christ’s exposure of sin is the prerequisite for its removal.
12: Adam’s blame-shifting — “the woman you put here with me—she gave me” — is the prototype of human refusal to own sin. The contrast with Christ is total: “God made him who had no sin to be sin for us” (2 Corinthians 5:21). Christ bears what he did not do; Adam refuses to bear what he did. The one who had every right to blame (he was innocent) took the blame; the one who had every reason to own his guilt (he was the sinner) deflected it. The exchange that Paul describes in 2 Corinthians 5:21 is the precise reversal of this scene.
13: “The serpent deceived me, and I ate.” Eve’s confession is more honest than Adam’s — she names both the deception and her own act. The church, though capable of Eve’s vulnerability to deception (2 Corinthians 11:3), is not left without defense: Christ has sent “the Spirit of truth” who guides into all truth (John 16:13) and who testifies to Christ (John 15:26). The “another Jesus” and “different gospel” that Paul warns about (2 Corinthians 11:4) are the ongoing form of the serpent’s deception; the antidote is the true Christ whom he proclaims.
14: The curse on the serpent — crawling on its belly, eating dust — is the beginning of the reversal that culminates in v15. The humiliation of the adversary begins here and ends at the cross: Christ, “having disarmed the powers and authorities… made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross” (Colossians 2:15). The serpent who stood over Eve to deceive is brought low; ultimately it will be thrown into the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10). The curse of v14 is the first chapter of the defeat that Christ completes.
16: The curse on the woman — pain in childbearing, distorted relationship with her husband — marks the disruption of the good creation order. Christ’s redemption addresses this: Paul uses the image of a woman’s labor pains for the travail of the new creation being born through the gospel (Romans 8:22; Galatians 4:19 — Paul “in the pains of childbirth until Christ is formed in you”). The specific pain of v16 is the ground from which the seed-of-the-woman (v15) will come. Galatians 4:4 — “born of a woman” — places the incarnation squarely in the world of the curse, the curse that Christ enters and redeems.
17: The curse on Adam and the ground — toil and futility — is the creation’s subjection that Paul describes in Romans 8:20: “the creation was subjected to frustration (mataiotēti), not by its own choice, but by the will of the one who subjected it, in hope.” The hope is the liberation that Christ brings: “the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay and brought into the freedom and glory of the children of God” (Romans 8:21). The curse of this verse is the condition from which creation waits for Christ’s redemption to free it.
18: “Thorns and thistles” — the curse on the ground produces the very material placed on Christ’s head at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:29; Mark 15:17; John 19:2). The crown of thorns is not accidental: Christ literally bears the curse of the fallen ground on his head, absorbing it in his body. Galatians 3:13 — “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us” — applies the same principle: he takes the curse on himself. The thorns that the cursed earth produces become the symbol of the curse Christ bears for the cursed.
19: “For dust you are and to dust you will return.” Death enters human experience as the fulfillment of God’s word (Genesis 2:17). Paul builds his entire resurrection theology on this verse and its reversal: “The first man was of the dust of the earth; the second man is of heaven. As was the earthly man, so are those who are of the earth; and as is the heavenly man, so also are those who are of heaven” (1 Corinthians 15:47–48). Christ takes on the dust-nature (“the Word became flesh”) in order to raise the dust-born to the heavenly. The death sentence of v19 is the sentence Christ undergoes and reverses in the resurrection.
20: Adam names his wife Eve (ḥawwāh), “because she would become the mother of all the living.” The name is an act of faith in the promise of v15: despite the death sentence just pronounced, Adam names her for life. The “mother of all the living” anticipates the woman whose seed will crush the serpent. In the NT, Christ himself is “the resurrection and the life” (John 11:25) and “the author of life” (Acts 3:15). The name Eve points through the biological mother of humanity to the one through whom true life comes — the seed she will bear across generations.
21: A type: “The LORD God made garments of skin for Adam and his wife and clothed them.” The garments of skin require the death of an animal — blood was shed to cover the shame of the guilty. This is the first sacrifice in the Bible and the first type of atonement: the innocent dies so the guilty can be clothed. The thread runs through the entire sacrificial system to Christ: “he has clothed me with garments of salvation and arrayed me in a robe of his righteousness” (Isaiah 61:10). Paul: “all of you who were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ” (Galatians 3:27). The blood that covers Adam’s shame is the type of the blood that covers ours.
22: “The man has now become like one of us, knowing good and evil.” The ironic divine assessment — the serpent’s promise was fulfilled, but at catastrophic cost. The divine concern is that the fallen image-bearer not now eat from the tree of life and “live forever” in that fallen state. Christ resolves this: he is the one who has perfectly united humanity and divinity without the corruption of sin (Hebrews 4:15 — “tempted in every way, just as we are — yet he did not sin”). Through Christ, the image-bearer is restored to share in divine life without the distortion of sin (2 Peter 1:4 — “you may participate in the divine nature”).
23: The exile from the garden — banished to work the very ground from which he was taken. The whole OT narrative of exile (from Canaan to Babylon) echoes this first exile, and the whole of redemptive history is the story of return from exile. Christ is the one who brings the final return: “I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me” (John 14:6). The exile from God’s presence is ended in Christ, who tears down the dividing wall (Ephesians 2:14) and gives access to the Father (Ephesians 2:18; Hebrews 10:19–22).
24: The cherubim and flaming sword guard the way to the tree of life — the access is barred. The entire redemptive narrative is about reopening this access. Christ removes the barrier: “To the one who is victorious, I will give the right to eat from the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God” (Revelation 2:7). The veil of the temple (which the cherubim motif decorated, Exodus 26:31) that guarded the holy of holies was torn from top to bottom at Christ’s death (Matthew 27:51), signaling that the way to God’s presence was open. Hebrews 10:20 calls Christ “a new and living way opened for us through the curtain, that is, his body” — the flaming sword that blocked access is removed by the one who bears it in his own death.
